# Visibility Before Compression

## Explains

Explains why systems must be made visible before they can be safely compressed or automated.

## The Concept

The sequence matters: first make visible and observable, then observe patterns, THEN compress into abstractions or automation. Reversing this sequence — compressing before understanding — creates problems that are invisible by definition. You cannot debug what you cannot see. You cannot safely abstract what you do not understand. You cannot reliably automate what you have not observed operating.

Compressing too early hides problems behind a layer of abstraction that becomes load-bearing before it is verified. Automating before understanding creates brittle black boxes that work until they do not, and when they fail, nobody understands why because nobody ever understood the original system well enough to compress it responsibly.

This applies universally. In governance: make rules visible (document them, surface them, show who they affect) before simplifying them. In engineering: do it manually first, observe the patterns, then automate the understood pattern. In organizations: understand the actual work being done before restructuring the org chart around an assumed model of that work. The framework's own descriptive-to-prescriptive-to-generative progression follows this principle — you must describe what exists before you can prescribe what should be, and you must prescribe successfully before you can generate automatically.

## Why This Matters for Governance

Governance is particularly susceptible to premature compression because governance designers often work from theory rather than observation. A rule created from first principles (compression without visibility) is less reliable than a rule that emerged from observing actual failure patterns (visibility first, then compression). JOS's design philosophy — start descriptive, become prescriptive only after observation — is this principle in action.

This also guides automation decisions. Before automating a governance check, first make it visible (show the check happening manually), observe the patterns (how often does it fail? what are the edge cases?), then compress into automation only after the behavior is well-understood.

## Connects To

- JOS-P15 (Prefer Observable Systems) — observability is the visibility that enables safe compression
- JOS-C08 (Compression Gradient) — the gradient defines how far compression has proceeded; this concept defines the prerequisite
- JOS-C17 (Graduated Automation) — automation is compression of human work; graduation ensures visibility precedes it

## Provenance

Foundry Evolution concept "Visibility First, Compression Later", reframed as a universal strategic sequencing principle. Related to the Lean principle of "go see" (genchi genbutsu) and the scientific method's insistence on observation before theorizing.
